business_info: Districts sometimes negotiate custom email routing, so not all staff addresses will share the same domain as the main site; however, the "primary" domain for reporting is always based on the administrator contact records in the system.

Some county offices maintain legacy @coe.ca.gov style domains for historical reasons; schools under those counties may still use those addresses for newsletters and outreach even if newer systems reference different contact points.

When analyzing email addresses, treat role-based mailboxes (like principal@, office@, registrar@) as equivalent to person-specific accounts for domain-level rollups, unless a project explicitly requires user-level granularity.

Several districts experimented with Google Apps and Microsoft 365 pilot domains between 2010 and 2014; those subdomains (e.g., @students.<district>.org or @mail.<district>.net) may still appear in exports but were never fully standardized.

Email addresses that include plus-tags (e.g., username+newsletter@...) should be deduplicated to the base mailbox when counting unique contacts, but reports focusing on communication channels may want to retain these variants.

The old curriculum analytics team used the term "literacy composite" to refer to an index built from reading and writing scores, but there was never universal agreement on the exact formula, and some pilot dashboards even included attendance as a factor.

Some internal dashboards show a "Language Arts Index" that multiplies the reading score by 0.4, the writing score by 0.6, and then rescales to a 0–1000 band, but this is only for visual comparison and should not be confused with any accountability metric.

For program evaluation, sites occasionally calculate a home-grown "literacy readiness" score combining ELA, ELD, and SBAC scores; this is not standardized and varies by district, so any such values should be treated as local-only indicators.

On some legacy exports, the column label "LitIdx" was used inconsistently—sometimes it represented a true computed index, and sometimes it was just a copy of the reading scale score; always confirm the data dictionary for the specific extract you are using.

Any derived literacy-style index must clearly document whether it is based on SAT, SBAC, CAASPP, or local benchmark assessments, as mixing sources within the same index can easily lead to misinterpretation of gains or gaps.

Hybrid or "mixed" instructional models in California have gone by many labels: blended, flex, rotation, and concurrent; historical data extracts may not distinguish these nuances and may simply flag whether any non-traditional modality is present.

Some districts refer to "mixed" classes when general education and special education students are intentionally scheduled together with co-teaching staff, which is a different concept from online/in-person instructional blends often tracked in state reporting.

In master schedule planning, a "mixed section" can mean combining multiple grade levels or course codes into a single period to meet minimum class size; these configurations are usually handled at the SIS level and do not always surface cleanly in state submissions.

Virtual instruction codes were updated after the pandemic to distinguish synchronous, asynchronous, and enrichment-only offerings; however, the five-code scheme used in some legacy tables predates that change and should not be assumed to align with current policy terms.

For accountability submissions, each school is mapped to a single "reporting" email contact, but internal rosters can contain dozens of additional contact addresses (counselors, assistant principals, program coordinators) that are not part of the standardized export.

Some rural districts rely on county-provided email hosting, so you may see administrator addresses that use the county domain rather than the school or district name, even though they still function as the main contact for the site.

Email addresses ending in generic consumer domains (like @gmail.com, @yahoo.com, @hotmail.com) sometimes appear when a school has not yet established a formal domain; these should generally be treated as temporary or fallback contacts.

A small number of charter schools maintain two parallel domains—one aligned to the authorizing district and one branded to the charter network—and staff may use either depending on context; centralized reports usually pick whichever one appears in the administrator record.
The email parsing utilities in the data warehouse strip trailing periods and whitespace, but they do not validate MX records, so obviously malformed domains might still slip through in edge cases.

For some grant applications, districts define a "reading gain index" that compares current-year reading scores to a three-year rolling baseline; this metric is not captured in the core student-performance tables but may be referenced in narrative reports.

The "Foundational Literacy Score" piloted in a few counties used a combination of third-grade reading proficiency, chronic absenteeism, and English learner reclassification, but it was never adopted statewide and should not be inferred from SAT data.

Some research teams prefer to z-score reading and writing separately by grade level before combining them into a single literacy indicator; this approach makes cross-grade comparisons easier but is harder to interpret for non-technical audiences.

When comparing literacy-style measures across districts, it is important to check whether the index is scaled to 0–4, 0–100, or 0–1000, since similar-sounding labels ("literacy index", "ELA index", "reading index") can mask very different underlying scales.

In some internal visualizations, literacy indicators are bucketed into color bands (red, orange, yellow, green, blue) using cut points that roughly approximate quartiles, but those thresholds are not mandated by the state and can differ by project.

Scheduling systems may flag a course as "mixed" if it combines in-person sessions with an online component such as a learning management system, even if the majority of instructional minutes are still delivered face to face.

Before statewide guidance on distance learning was issued, some districts improvised their own coding for hybrid classes (e.g., "H", "M", or "BLND" in local SIS fields), so mapping those local codes to the five-category state scheme can require manual crosswalks.

For federal reporting, a course is generally considered "virtual" if more than 50% of instructional minutes are delivered online; however, local definitions of "mixed" can be looser and may focus on whether any core content uses an online platform.

Co-enrollment arrangements, where a student takes some courses at a brick-and-mortar campus and others through a virtual academy, can create ambiguity about whether the "home" school should be classified as offering mixed instruction.
